'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in qq93 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in qq93 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in qq93 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in qq93 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)|1=Shōen}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)|1=Shōen}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

revision.text:
'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|12=draft}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市|12=draft}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町|12=draft}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|12=draft}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)|1=Shōen|12=draft}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|12=draft}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|12=draft}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

<!--Ōno Clan (Higo Province)--> <!--Ōno Clan (Higo Province)-->

'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)|1=Shōen|qq=draft|qq=draft}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

revision.comment: 25 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:大野氏 (肥後国) up to rev 99737614
revision.text:
大野氏（肥後国）は、日本の氏族。[[平安時代]]～[[安土桃山時代]]まで肥後国[[大野別府]]（現在の[[熊本県]][[玉名市]]、旧[[岱明町]]辺り）の[[荘官]]、[[地頭]]。
== 概要 ==
* 大野氏の氏寺として天長元年(824)に加善大徳が寿福寺を開山。寿福寺は天台宗比叡山[[延暦寺]]の末寺で後の繁根木(はねぎ)八幡宮神宮寺。現[[玉名市図書館]]近辺。 * 紀大野隆村（きのおおのたかむら）は、応和元年(961)、第62代・村上天皇の[[勅願]]で山城国 [[石清水八幡宮]]を歓請し現在の熊本県玉名市、繁根木(はねぎ)八幡宮を創建、[[大野別府]]250町歩(250ヘクタール)の総鎮守にした。
* [[大野別府]]は、大野荘とも呼ばれる[[荘園 (日本)]]で領主は紀姓の大野氏。領家は福岡の[[筥崎宮]](筥崎八幡宮)。本家は京都の[[石清水八幡宮]]。
* [[紀氏]]の紀国隆(きのくにたか)は建久4年(1193)[[大野別府]]の地頭。
* 国隆の子、大野十郎秀隆は大野氏の居城であった[[日嶽城]](ひだけじょう)の城主。[[惣領]]職。秀隆の兄弟は中村、築地(ついじ)、河崎、中尾、岩崎、二塚(山田)、尾崎氏となった。 * 大野小次郎、大野岩崎太郎、大野田十郎は弘安4年(1281)[[元寇]]、弘安の役に出陣、奮戦した。戦後、[[蒙古]]合戦勲功賞で田畑、屋敷の恩賞を得た。 * 鎌倉～室町時代(1185～1573)にかけて[[大野別府]]の貿易港、高瀬津(港)から中国大陸(アモイ、マカオ)、東南アジア(バンコク、マニラ)などと幅広い交易を行った。
* 大野氏は[[南北朝時代 (日本)]](1336～1392)、[[南朝 (日本)]]方として[[菊池氏]]と共に動いた。 * 大野伊勢守光隆、大野式部大輔乗資は正平14年(1359,北朝年号延文4年)[[菊池武光]]と共に日本三大合戦にも数えられる[[筑後川の戦い]]、大保原合戦(おおほばるかっせん）に出陣。 * 大野出羽守朝隆(光隆の子)は応安10年(1377)5月3日、[[日嶽城]]城主となった。応永10年(1403)[[大野別府]]領内の寿福寺、静空御坊 鎮慶に河崎名 向津留(むこうづる)不動堂の免畠([[免田]])を寄進した。 * 大野紀重門は天文7年(1538)[[大野別府]]領内の繁根木八幡宮 再建の[[檀那]]。
* [[大野別府]]の北側に隣接する北朝方の野原荘、地頭小代氏は1581年3月、肥前の[[龍造寺隆信]]の命で、[[日嶽城]]、高道城、上村城を攻めた(金山原の合戦)。[[日嶽城]] 城主 大野弾正親祐、大野左馬介は一族挙げて奮戦するも4倍以上の小代勢力に多くが討ち死。生き残った大野一族は南へ逃れた。その後、大野左馬介は1589年、[[熊本藩]]藩主となる[[加藤清正]]の家臣(俸禄３百石)となった。 * 大野左馬介の子、大野新助は旧[[大野別府]]領内の現[[玉名市]]滑石へ復帰。新助-大野四郎兵衛 親秀(天和元年 1681年卒)-大野次左衛門親昌と続き、親昌の3子が滑石奥屋敷、中屋敷、表屋敷に分かれた。親昌の兄、大野源八は寛文5年(1665)御物頭役(俸禄2百石)で[[細川氏]]に仕えて分家した。 * 旧滑石村の[[庄屋]]、奥屋敷の大野利平次の次男、大野十左衛門一隆は寛政元年(1789)より大規模な[[干拓]]、用水路開発事業(大野牟田)を行い、その功により[[熊本藩]](肥後細川藩)武士の役職の一つ「諸役人段」に列せられた。 * 旧滑石村の大野一則(奥屋敷から分家の小路屋敷)は昭和3年(1928)「玉名郡大地主調」で徳米俵数2000/年。 * [[大野別府]]があった旧[[岱明町]](現[[玉名市]])の町長、大野金吉は小学校教頭、校長などを務めていた門岡 久氏に明治百年記念の[[岱明町]]事業として『岱明町地方史』執筆を要請し、昭和44年(1969)に発行された。
=== 出典 ===
* 『関東下知状案』貞応3年5月21日 * 『大野朝隆寄進状』(寿福寺文書) 応永10年5月13日 大野出羽守朝隆 * 『熊本県玉名郡誌』大正12年4月18日 * 『岱明町地方史』昭和44年7月4日 門岡 久著 * 『根小屋式山城 日嶽城 -中世の山城日嶽城跡・古城跡調査報告-』平成6年3月1日 岱明町文化保護委員会(玉名歴史研究会『歴史玉名』第16号 抜刷)
{{DEFAULTSORT:おおのし}} [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:九州地方の氏族]]

[[sn:Ōno Clan (Higo Province)]] [[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]] [[ja:Ōno Clan (Higo Province)]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Ono Clan (Higo Province)}}
<!--Ōno Clan (Higo Province)--> <!--Ōno Clan (Higo Province)--> {{translated page|ja|大野氏 (肥後国)|version=99737614|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)|1=Shōen|qq=draft|qq=draft}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)
{{DEFAULTSORT:Ono Clan (Higo Province)}}

== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

revision.text:
'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuchi–Momoyama period|qq=}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana City|ja|玉名市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City|qq=}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei Town|ja|岱明町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taimei Town|qq=}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|qq=}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen (manor)|ja|荘園 (日本)|1=Shōen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen (manor)|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōen|qq=}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|qq=}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanboku-chō period|qq=}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.

Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. * In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] to the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day [[:en:Tamana, Kumamoto|Tamana City]], Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former [[:en:Taimei, Kumamoto|Taimei Town]] area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|qq=}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|qq=}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day [[:en:Tamana, Kumamoto|Tamana City]], Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former [[:en:Taimei, Kumamoto|Taimei Town]] area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|qq=|1=Tamana City Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572420}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|qq=|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)
{{translated page|ja|大野氏 (肥後国)|version=99737614|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day [[:en:Tamana, Kumamoto|Tamana City]], Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former [[:en:Taimei, Kumamoto|Taimei Town]] area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|qq=|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.

Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|qq=|1=Tamana City Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572420}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|qq=|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] to the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day [[:en:Tamana, Kumamoto|Tamana City]], Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former [[:en:Taimei, Kumamoto|Taimei Town]] area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|qq=|1=Tamana City Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572420}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|qq=|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

[[en:Ōno Clan (Higo Province)]] {{wikidata link|Q136731905}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] to the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day [[:en:Tamana, Kumamoto|Tamana City]], Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former [[:en:Taimei, Kumamoto|Taimei Town]] area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|1=Tamana City Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572420}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)
