His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

revision.comment: Add page with conflicting Wikidata QIDs
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] to the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day [[:en:Tamana, Kumamoto|Tamana City]], Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former [[:en:Taimei, Kumamoto|Taimei Town]] area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|1=Tamana City Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572420}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a [[:en:Shōen|Shōen]] estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]] (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen|en|Shōen|WD=Q1887569|lt=Shōen}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|1=Hidake Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大野氏 (肥後国)]]
'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana, Kumamoto|en|Tamana, Kumamoto|WD=Q861610|lt=Tamana City|1=Tamana, Kumamoto}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei, Kumamoto|en|Taimei, Kumamoto|WD=Q7676158|lt=Taimei Town|1=Taimei, Kumamoto}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|1=Q11572420|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572420}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen|en|Shōen|WD=Q1887569|lt=Shōen|1=Shōen}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|1=Q132125276|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)

revision.text:
'''Ōno Clan (Higo Province)''' (大野氏) was a Japanese clan based in the Higo Province, active from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} to the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. The clan served as local officials and land stewards (shokan and jito) in the Ōno Beppu region, corresponding to present-day {{ill|Tamana, Kumamoto|en|Tamana, Kumamoto|WD=Q861610|lt=Tamana City|1=Tamana, Kumamoto}}, Kumamoto Prefecture, particularly the former {{ill|Taimei, Kumamoto|en|Taimei, Kumamoto|WD=Q7676158|lt=Taimei Town|1=Taimei, Kumamoto}} area.
== Overview ==
* The Ōno clan's family temple, Jufuku-ji, was established in 824 (Tenchō 1) by Kazen Daishi. Originally affiliated with the Tendai sect’s Enryaku-ji, it later became the Shingon sect's Hanegi Hachiman Shrine Jingūji. The temple was located near the present {{ill|Tamana City Library|ja|玉名市図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamana City Library|1=Q11572420|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572420}}. * Kii Ōno Takamura (紀大野隆村) founded the Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 961 (Ōwa 1) by imperial decree from Emperor Murakami. This shrine became the guardian shrine of Ōno Beppu, covering 250 chō (approximately 250 hectares). * Ōno Beppu, also known as Ōno Shō, was a {{ill|Shōen|en|Shōen|WD=Q1887569|lt=Shōen|1=Shōen}} estate governed by the Ōno clan of the Kii lineage. Its lord was the Hakozaki Shrine in Fukuoka, and its principal shrine was the Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine in Kyoto.
* Kii Kunitaka (紀国隆) served as the steward (jito) of Ōno Beppu in 1193 (Kenkyū 4). * His son, Ōno Jūrō Hidekuni, was the lord of {{ill|Hidake Castle|ja|日嶽城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidake Castle|1=Q132125276|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132125276}}, the clan's stronghold. As the clan head, he held the role of sōryō (chief retainer). His brothers founded branches of the clan, becoming the Nakamura, Tsui-ji, Kawasaki, Nakao, Iwasaki, Futatsuka (Yamada), and Ozaki clans. * Members of the clan, including Ōno Kojiro, Ōno Iwasaki Tarō, and Ōno Den Jūrō, fought valiantly in the Mongol invasions of Japan (1281, Kōan 4). Afterward, they received rewards of land and estates.
* During the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} (1336–1392), the Ōno clan supported the Southern Court alongside the Kikuchi clan. * Ōno Ise no Kami Mitsutaka and Ōno Shikibu no Taifu Norisuke fought in the Battle of Chikugo River (Daihobaru Kassen) in 1359 (Shōhei 14, Northern Court year Enbun 4), one of Japan’s three major battles, alongside Kikuchi Takemitsu. * Ōno Dewa no Kami Tomotaka, son of Mitsutaka, became the lord of Hidake Castle on May 3, 1377 (Ōan 10). In 1403 (Ōei 10), he donated tax-exempt fields to Jufuku-ji Temple and the Mukōdzuru Fudō-dō Hall within Ōno Beppu.
* Ōno Kunitakado was the patron for rebuilding Hanegi Hachiman Shrine in 1538 (Tenbun 7).
* In 1581, the forces of Chōshiro commanded by Ryūzōji Takanobu attacked Hidake Castle, Takamichi Castle, and Kamimura Castle during the Battle of Kanayabaru. The Ōno family fought valiantly, but many were killed. Survivors fled south. Later, Ōno Samanosuke became a retainer of Katō Kiyomasa, lord of the Kumamoto Domain, receiving a stipend of 300 koku in 1589.
* The descendants of Ōno Samanosuke's son, Ōno Shinsuke, returned to their ancestral lands in what is now Tamana City, Kumamoto Prefecture. His descendants divided into three branches: Okuyashiki, Nakayashiki, and Omoteyashiki. Meanwhile, Ōno Genpachi, the elder brother of Shinsuke, served the Hosokawa clan with a stipend of 200 koku in 1665 (Kanbun 5).
* In 1789 (Kansei 1), Ōno Juzaemon Ichitaka initiated a major reclamation project, creating irrigation systems known as Ōnomuta. For this achievement, he was granted a bureaucratic Rank within the Kumamoto Domain. * In 1928 (Shōwa 3), Ōno Ichinori, a landowner from Okuyashiki, was listed in the Tamana County Landlord Registry with an annual rice yield of 2,000 koku.
* The mayor of Taimei Town, Ōno Kinkichi, collaborated with local educators to commission and publish the "Taimei Town Local History" in 1969 as part of a centennial project commemorating the Meiji Restoration.
== Sources == * "Kantō Kudashijō-an" (May 21, Jōō 3) * "Ōno Tomotaka Donation Letter" (Ōei 10, Jufuku-ji Temple Documents) * "History of Tamana County, Kumamoto Prefecture" (April 18, 1923) * "Taimei Town Local History" (July 4, 1969, by Kisa Kado) * "Reports on Medieval Castles in Taimei" (March 1, 1994, Taimei Town Cultural Preservation Committee)
